skill.name: binary-analysis-patterns
skill.description: Reference guide for recognizing common binary analysis patterns in assembly, control flow, data structures, decompilation, Ghidra, and IDA Pro workflows.
skill.summary: Identify binary analysis patterns for static reverse engineering work.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command and weak cryptography patterns, but review found they are Markdown examples and keyword collisions, not executable skill behavior. No runnable scripts, network calls, filesystem access, credential access, or prompt injection attempts were found in SKILL.md. The skill is safe to publish as a documentation-only binary analysis reference.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Markdown Code Examples
low_findings.description: The external command findings are false positives from fenced Markdown examples that show assembly, C, Java, and Python snippets for reverse engineering education. SKILL.md contains reference text and code examples, but no runnable skill script or instruction that executes these snippets automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched regions are inside Markdown code fences or prose examples. No evidence found that the skill invokes a shell, Ruby backticks, subprocesses, or other execution mechanisms.

low_findings.title: Dismissed Static Finding: Weak Crypto Keyword Collision
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings are false positives. The referenced lines contain the skill description and a memcpy destination operand, not a DES implementation or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Semantic review confirms these lines do not define, call, or recommend weak cryptography. The signal appears to come from ordinary text or operand names, so the finding is highly likely to be false positive.

content.value_statement: Binary analysis can be slow when assembly patterns are unfamiliar. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured reference for static reverse engineering workflows.
actual_capabilities: Explains common x86-64, ARM64, and ARM32 instruction patterns.
Maps assembly snippets to control flow constructs such as branches, loops, and switch statements.
Identifies array, structure, linked list, string, arithmetic, and bit manipulation patterns.
Guides function signature, variable, and type recovery during decompilation.
Provides Ghidra and IDA Pro analysis tips for naming, typing, references, and pattern searches.
limitations: Does not execute binary analysis tools or inspect binaries by itself.
Does not include malware detonation, dynamic analysis, or sandbox procedures.
Does not replace expert validation for obfuscated, packed, or hostile binaries.
Provides general patterns, not architecture manuals or complete tool documentation.

use_cases.title: Triage Unknown Functions
use_cases.description: Use pattern checklists to infer calling conventions, stack frames, control flow, and likely function behavior.

use_cases.title: Improve Decompiler Output
use_cases.description: Recover probable variables, types, structures, and signatures before renaming symbols in Ghidra or IDA Pro.

use_cases.title: Teach Assembly Recognition
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain how source-level constructs appear in compiled code.

prompt_templates.title: Classify a Snippet
prompt_templates.prompt: Identify the likely source-level construct represented by this assembly snippet. Explain the key instructions and confidence level.

prompt_templates.title: Recover Function Intent
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this function listing for calling convention, parameters, local variables, return value, and probable purpose.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Decompiled Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this decompiler output and assembly context. Suggest better names, types, structures, and comments with reasoning.

prompt_templates.title: Build an Analysis Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a static analysis plan for this binary. Include triage steps, pattern checks, tooling actions, and verification notes.

output_examples.input: A function starts with a saved frame pointer, uses register arguments, and returns through the accumulator register.
output_examples.output: The output would identify a standard function prologue, infer the calling convention, describe likely parameters, and list evidence for the return value.

output_examples.input: A decompiler shows pointer arithmetic with repeated eight-byte offsets and cross-references from several functions.
output_examples.output: The output would suggest a structure or linked list pattern, propose field names, and recommend validation through references and memory layout.

output_examples.input: An assembly block compares one value against several constants before jumping to labeled cases.
output_examples.output: The output would classify the block as a switch or chained conditional pattern and explain how to confirm the default branch.

best_practices: Start with file type, architecture, imports, exports, strings, and entry points before deep function work.
Record evidence for each recovered type, name, and control flow decision.
Validate decompiler assumptions against raw assembly when optimization or obfuscation may affect output.
anti_patterns: Do not trust decompiler output without checking key assembly instructions.
Do not rename functions from one weak string reference without cross-reference validation.
Do not assume optimized binaries preserve source-level structure.

faq.question: Does this skill run disassemblers or decompilers?
faq.answer: No. It is a reference guide for analysis patterns and workflows.

faq.question: Which architectures are covered?
faq.answer: It covers x86-64, ARM64, and ARM32 examples at a pattern level.

faq.question: Can it help with Ghidra or IDA Pro?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes practical guidance for signatures, structures, references, naming, and pattern searches.

faq.question: Is this a malware analysis skill?
faq.answer: It can support static malware review, but it does not provide sandboxing or dynamic analysis procedures.

faq.question: Can beginners use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The examples explain common patterns, but users still need basic assembly knowledge.

faq.question: Does it include prompt injection or hidden instructions?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file contains normal Markdown guidance and code examples.
